title: Security controls require regression lock
description: A security control without a focused regression test or reviewable evidence is likely to regress during later refactors.
body: Use after fixing authz, injection, secret, or supply-chain problems. Do not use to demand exhaustive test suites for documentation-only changes. Actions: add the smallest negative test that captures the old failure, name the protected behavior, and record the command used. Validation signals include the test failing before the fix or a review note explaining why that was impossible. Risk boundary: high-risk fixes without regression lock need human acceptance of residual risk. Use when Security Risk Controls work touches trust boundaries, authorization decisions, dangerous sinks, secrets, and dependency execution. Do not use this guidance to override a more specific pack, user instruction, or repository contract. Actions: use the fact to constrain the change and test plan; inspect the current files first, keep the change scoped to the owning boundary, and record what evidence changed the decision. Validation signals include negative security tests, server-side checks, sink payload tests, and secret scans. Risk boundary: escalate or stop when the task involves client-only controls, injection, credential leakage, permissive fallback, and unsafe dependency changes, missing owner evidence, or live external state.
